Putting together roadmap for CLM/irisk to float 6.16.26 KK - devika introduced me - asked for time to connect and invited to palo alto event 6.9.26 AB - Sent a transition note to Peter. Decision pushed out until mid- July. Down to 1 of 2 vendors including Ironclad. 5.26.26 KC: Candence leadership meeting this week to listen to final budget requests. CLM project led (peter Hughes) indicated confirmation and vendor selection anticiapted first week of June. 5.15.26 KC: Wrap up meeting planned for 5.20 @ 10am PT. 5.4.26 KC: CLM POC kickoff 5.12 with subsequent daily reviews and Q&A. 4.28.26 KC: Submit revised POC with expanded timeline (Completed) Deliver an answer regarding support document volumes (Hemant) (Completed) Secure 2nd reference (Completed - Ahod chosen) 4.21.26 KC: Submit MSA. (Completed)
hard.ais_why:
• The 95-minute 4/3/2026 workshop generated 22 action items. Devika Sanil's commitment to send Jason Lieu and the team the list of external data sources behind the AI risk model is a buyer-pulled ask, and Kevin Cipollaro's cost breakdown is split into CLM, Intake, and iRisk line items, confirming Merlin Intake and AI-driven iRisk are inside the priced scope.
• Other buyer-pulled commitments include the SAP adapter intro to Devesh Chadha, sub-tier mapping plus FedRAMP authorization status, the product roadmap going to Jason and Devesh, and a standard-versus-premium support table. Cadence is driving the agenda. The earlier 1/25/2026 rep note that Cadence is unimpressed with Coupa's Agentic AI is the competitive opening Zycus is now stepping into.
• POC kickoff is scheduled for 5/12/2026, the MSA went in on 4/21, two references provided, and the project lead has told the team Zycus is rated higher than the other vendor. Transcript-level keyword review confirmed AI and Merlin signal present; champion, POC scope including Intake and iRisk, and budget alignment justify High confidence.

north_star_verdict.headline: Healthy, deeply-engaged two-finalist deal (Zycus vs Ironclad) where the project lead has told his team Zycus is rated higher, but the 10 Jul close date is not credible: the buyer's own 9 Jun Next Step pushed the decision to mid-July and on 19 Jun they said they have everything they need and will reach out, so expect a 2-4 week forward slip. The win turns on converting Peter Hughes into an advocate and getting the budget approver briefed before selection.
north_star_verdict.math: Close date 2026-07-10 is 4 days out (today 2026-07-06). Stage Shortlisted since ~25 Jan (~5.5 months). Last activity 2026-07-02 is a rep-side email awaiting reply; last genuine buyer inbound 19 Jun ('we have all the information we need from Zycus and will reach out'). Per the 9 Jun Next Step the decision moved to mid-July with the field down to 1 of 2 incl. Ironclad, so vendor selection, leadership budget sign-off, MSA redlines and SOW cannot complete by 10 Jul: a slip to late Jul / early Aug is required.
north_star_verdict.recommended_forecast: Pipeline until vendor selection and leadership budget sign-off land. Best Case is not yet defensible: no budget-approving executive has engaged on any call and Peter Hughes has not yet advocated Zycus over Ironclad in writing.
evidence: Salesforce Next Step (9 Jun, Alexa Bradley): decision pushed to mid-July, down to 1 of 2 vendors including Ironclad.
Salesforce activity (19 Jun inbound email): 'we have all the information we need from Zycus and will reach out' with next steps - a waiting, slightly cooled state.
Salesforce Next Step (26 May): Cadence leadership meeting to hear final budget requests; vendor selection then anticipated first week of June (since slipped).
AIS_Why (project lead): Cadence has told the team Zycus is rated higher than the other vendor - a positive competitive lean that is not yet locked.

metrics.narrative: Cadence has a clear volume anchor but no dollar ROI. On the 13 Apr pricing/volume sync Peter Hughes referenced an internal estimate near 12,500 contracts a year and a legacy repository of roughly 500,000 documents to migrate, and on the 28 Apr POC-framework call Melanie Hamm flagged migration complexity across iManage plus documents pulled from the relevant stakeholder is one of Cadence's lower-TCV product lines, so Melanie stated on 21 Apr that 'efficiency is really key for margin' - the qualitative value driver. No cost-per-contract, cycle-time, or headcount-savings figure has been quantified, so the C-level dollar case is still open; quantifying time-saved-per-contract times ~12,500 contracts is the move.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has appeared on any recorded call. Devesh Chadha (Director - Procurement COE, Decision Maker on the contact roles) controls the RFP, budget conversation and timeline and drove the procurement line-item and Peppol requirements on the 28 Apr P2P call, but he is a decision maker, not the signing authority on a $210k five-year commitment. The 26 May Next Step names a 'Cadence leadership meeting to listen to final budget requests', confirming a leadership budget layer above Devesh that has never engaged Zycus directly, and the 19 Jun buyer email deflected the executive-connect offer. The single biggest power gap is a brokered 30-minute briefing with that budget approver before mid-July.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are well documented from the use-case workshops and POC. On 21 Apr Melanie Hamm defined the make-or-break sell-side criterion as complex coded-template generation ('the coded template understands... pull that answer, put it into this part of the template') plus admin-side ease of maintenance without IT release cycles ('I have to go through a release date and testing and IT, and that's what I'm trying to get away from'). On 28 Apr Devesh Chadha added procurement criteria: line-item and pricing-schedule intake, Peppol validated-invoice processing for Belgium/France/Italy, and PO-consumption data flowing back from P2P. Cross-cutting criteria are one platform across procurement, legal and sales, Merlin AI intake, and strict InfoSec. Two open product gaps (complex coded-template removal logic and sales-vs-legal access control) remain Zycus's to close.

decision_process.narrative: The process is a formal CLM RFP that has run through demos (Mar-Apr), a use-case workshop series, parallel 5-day POC sandboxes for both finalists in early-to-mid May, and now a leadership budget-and-selection gate. Read from the call sequence, Peter Hughes orchestrates and gates access, Melanie Hamm owns the legal/sell-side evaluation, Jason Lieu owns sourcing/POC logistics, and Devesh Chadha owns procurement requirements and the RFP. The 26 May Next Step put vendor selection first week of June; the 9 Jun Next Step slipped it to mid-July after a leadership budget meeting, and on 19 Jun the buyer said they have everything they need. The approval chain runs Peter/Devesh up to an unnamed Cadence leadership budget owner.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting is partially staged but not visibly advancing. The MSA was submitted 21 Apr (Completed per Next Step) to seed parallel legal review, and InfoSec/architecture diligence advanced on the 6 Apr IT session. Cadence is a second-tier defense contractor with hard InfoSec obligations, and migration scope (iManage plus Salesforce-sourced documents) is a live workstream Melanie Hamm is still sizing. However, no redline or SOW-scoping session appears on any call after 28 Apr, and the named legal reviewers (Melanie Hamm, Dave Bebb, Issac Lin) have not surfaced open redline points. With a mid-July decision, contracting is the most likely execution tie-break; booking a legal-plus-procurement redline/SOW sync on selection is the move to de-risk it.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed and specific. Cadence runs a fragmented sell-side stack (Salesforce intake, iManage repository, Microsoft Word coded templates maintained by IT, DocuSign) and Coupa on the buy-side, with no unified CLM orchestration. On 21 Apr Melanie Hamm described the core pain as brittle IT-owned coded-template generation that forces a 'release date and testing and IT' cycle for every change, on a low-TCV product line where 'efficiency is really key for margin'. On 28 Apr Devesh Chadha described procurement pain around line-item/pricing codification and PO-utilization tracking across systems. Pain owners: legal/contracts (Melanie), procurement (Devesh/Jason), and sales-finance users.

champion.narrative: Peter Hughes (Senior Category Manager, the CLM project lead and primary contact) is warming from coach toward champion. He orchestrates every session, drove the sandbox-access ask, was personally introduced by the relevant stakeholder on 16 Jun and invited to the Palo Alto event, and - per the AIS_Why - has told his team Zycus is rated higher than the other vendor, the first real advocacy signal. What is still missing: he has not brokered an introduction to the budget approver, has not shared a written head-to-head POC scorecard, and the 19 Jun 'we have all we need, we'll reach out' note came back from his side, a passive posture heading into selection. Convert him with a POC debrief and an approver intro; Melanie Hamm and Jason Lieu are supporting influencers, not advocates.

competition.narrative: This is a two-finalist bake-off, Zycus vs Ironclad, confirmed by the 9 Jun Next Step ('down to 1 of 2 vendors including Ironclad'); both ran parallel POC sandboxes in early May. Ironclad is the single live threat, strong on legal-AI brand and admired by Cadence's legal team on the 23 Jan intro call. The displaced incumbents are Coupa (buy-side CLM), the homegrown Salesforce/iManage/Word sell-side stack, and DocuSign (e-signature). Zycus's wedge is enterprise breadth - one platform across procurement, legal and sales with Merlin AI intake and native Coupa/SAP/Salesforce integration - versus Ironclad's legal-only depth, and the project lead already rates Zycus higher. The gap: that breadth has not been carried to the budget approver and no written post-POC scorecard has been obtained.

deal_movement.summary: Reads the pulse from Next_Step history, field history and the last real buyer touch: a healthy two-finalist deal that slipped its decision from first-week-June to mid-July and entered a quiet buyer-waiting window, with a Zycus AE handoff mid-cycle.

items.change: Decision pushed to mid-July; field down to 1 of 2 vendors including Ironclad
Close date moved from 26 Jun to 10 Jul
Buyer said they have all the information they need from Zycus and will reach out with next steps
Zycus introduced new AE Karson Keogh to Peter Hughes; Peter invited Zycus to the Palo Alto event
Ownership transferred from Kevin Cipollaro to Karson Keogh (Zycus-side handoff)
Karson assembling a CLM/iRisk roadmap to float to the team

competitive_position.summary: Two-finalist bake-off, Zycus vs Ironclad, confirmed by the 9 Jun Next Step, with the project lead telling his team Zycus is rated higher than the other vendor (AIS_Why) - Zycus is currently ahead but not selected. Ironclad is the single live threat (high), strong on legal-AI brand and admired by Cadence's legal team on 23 Jan. The displaced incumbents - Coupa buy-side, the homegrown Salesforce/iManage/Word sell-side stack, and DocuSign - are low/dormant. Zycus's edge is enterprise breadth vs Ironclad's legal-only depth; it is not yet activated with the budget approver.

competitors.name: Ironclad
competitors.quote: Legal team has pressing AI requirements and has been impressed by tools like iClad
competitors.source: Avoma intro call 23 Jan 2026 + SF Next Step 9 Jun 2026
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as one enterprise platform across procurement, legal and sales with Merlin AI and Teams intake; frame Ironclad as a legal-only point solution that leaves procurement and sales fragmented, and carry the project lead's higher rating to the budget approver.

competitors.name: Coupa (buy-side CLM incumbent)
competitors.quote: Currently using Coupa for CLM, he's seeking one platform to manage both buy-side vendor contracts and sell-side legal/sales agreements
competitors.how_we_win: Unify buy-side and sell-side on one platform Coupa cannot serve across legal and sales; integrate to Coupa sourcing rather than rip it out.

competitors.name: Homegrown Salesforce/iManage/Word (sell-side)
competitors.quote: Our IT people have that coded template... I have to go through a release date and testing and IT, and that's what I'm trying to get away from
competitors.how_we_win: Replace brittle IT-owned coded templates with admin-configurable, metadata-driven clause automation the legal team maintains without IT release cycles.

competitors.name: DocuSign (e-signature incumbent)
competitors.quote: DocuSign is the de facto e-signature solution
competitors.how_we_win: Integrate with DocuSign rather than displace it; not a competitive blocker.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong fit on the enterprise-breadth and AI-intake criteria and confirmed on the priced iRisk/Intake scope; the two exposed points are complex coded-template generation and sales-vs-legal access control, both Zycus product items still open.

items.criterion: One platform across procurement, legal and sales with Merlin AI intake
items.quote: There is the Merlin intake application... an AI based guided assistance
The removing component, Melanie, is not part of this capability... I'll talk to my product team

items.criterion: Admin maintainability without IT release cycles
items.quote: 95 to 98% of our admin changes are without any IT dependencies

items.criterion: Procurement line-item / pricing-schedule intake and PO-consumption tracking
items.quote: We do have the APIs to intake it. I'll still reconfirm that the line items are included as part of the API

items.criterion: Sales-vs-legal access control for contract initiation
items.quote: I'll still confirm with my product team if the access in Salesforce can be bifurcated

items.requirement: Confirm whether complex coded-template remove-logic can be supported
items.quote: The difference of a coded template is it's not just adding stuff... it's removing things instead of adding things

items.requirement: Confirm Peppol validated-invoice processing for Belgium, France and Italy with a short demo
items.quote: Could you find out... and if they can, I'd like to see a 15 minute demo

items.deliverable: Confirm with product whether complex coded-template generation logic can be delivered
items.grounding_quote: I'll talk to my product team... worst case if we do find a gap, we build that logic in the Zycus template library

items.deliverable: Confirm sales-vs-legal access-control bifurcation via Salesforce initiation
items.grounding_quote: I'll still confirm with my product team if the access in Salesforce can be bifurcated

items.deliverable: Provide contract-vs-supporting-document migration volumes from iManage and Salesforce
items.grounding_quote: They have to go figure out how to get me that information... we need to be pulling some documents from Salesforce as well, not just iManage

items.quote: Cadence leadership meeting this week to listen to final budget requests

items.area: No written post-POC head-to-head scorecard vs Ironclad
items.quote: we have all the information we need from Zycus and will reach out

best_practice_check.summary: Mid-stage two-finalist deal where Zycus is narrowly ahead; the win levers are champion conversion, power access, and closing two open product gaps before selection.
flags: Convert Peter Hughes from coach to champion: debrief the POC and get him to broker the budget-approver intro before mid-July - he already rates Zycus higher but has not carried it to power.
Close the two open product gaps (complex coded-template logic, sales-vs-legal access control) in writing so they cannot become Ironclad's wedge at selection.
Multi-thread to the unnamed leadership budget owner while breadth is Zycus's edge; no budget-approving executive has engaged on any call.

Still missing: a brokered budget-approver introduction, a written head-to-head POC scorecard, and active push-back against Ironclad. The 19 Jun 'we have all we need, we'll reach out' posture keeps him at developing, not confirmed.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI/Merlin intake and iRisk are inside the priced scope and were central across the workshops and POC; the agentic story is landing and is Zycus's breadth wedge over Ironclad's legal-only depth.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 8.0 (Strong) is corroborated by the calls: Merlin Intake and AI-driven iRisk are in the priced scope, the 4/3 workshop generated 22 buyer-pulled AI action items, and the legal team has pressing AI requirements (23 Jan). Not over-rated.

items.detail: No budget-approving executive has engaged Zycus; the leadership budget/selection layer above Devesh Chadha is unmapped ahead of a mid-July decision.
items.source: SF Next Step 26 May 2026 + activity 19 Jun 2026

items.detail: Two open product gaps - complex coded-template remove-logic and sales-vs-legal access control - remain uncommitted and could become Ironclad's wedge at selection.
Decision slipped from first-week-June to mid-July and the 10 Jul close date is not credible; buyer is in a quiet waiting window since 19 Jun.
items.source: SF Next Step 9 Jun 2026 + activity 19 Jun 2026

confidence_signals.summary: Deal is live but cooled into a buyer-waiting window: the last genuine buyer touch was 19 Jun ('we have all we need, we'll reach out'), the 2 Jul touch was a rep-side email awaiting reply, and a mid-cycle AE handoff added friction. Not dark - a healthy final-two deal awaiting a mid-July selection.

items.expected_effect: Confirms our lead, exposes any Ironclad gap to close, and converts Peter from coach to advocate before the decision.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Zycus is the narrowly-ahead finalist in a two-horse CLM race (Zycus vs Ironclad) where the project lead has told his team Zycus is rated higher; the only things between us and the win are an unengaged budget approver and two open product gaps before a slipped, mid-July decision.

win_position.text: Why this number: a Shortlisted final-two deal earns roughly 60 - strong product fit, AIS 8/10, and the project lead saying Zycus is rated higher than the other vendor lift it, but it holds below 70 because no budget-approving executive is engaged and Peter Hughes has not advocated Zycus over Ironclad in writing.
Cadence is down to 1 of 2 vendors including Ironclad and the project lead has told his team Zycus is rated higher, per the 9 Jun Next Step and the AIS_Why note.
Ironclad is a live legal-AI threat admired by Cadence's legal team on 23 Jan and ran a parallel POC, so the lead is real but not locked.
No economic buyer has engaged: the 26 May leadership budget meeting and the 19 Jun 'we have all we need' email show power sitting above Devesh Chadha, out of reach of Zycus.

deal_momentum.text: Momentum cooled: the decision slipped from first-week-June to mid-July (9 Jun) and the buyer went to a wait state on 19 Jun saying they will reach out.
An AE handoff from Kevin Cipollaro to Karson Keogh on 14 Jun mid-decision added re-introduction friction with Peter Hughes.
Commercial and legal topics are live - MSA submitted 21 Apr, pricing/support reviewed 7 Apr, two references provided - so the deal is deep, not early.

customer_commitment.text: Cadence pulled 22 action items from the 3 Apr workshop, ran a full 5-day POC sandbox, provided two references, and accepted the MSA for review - concrete investment on their side.
Commitment is capped by a passive posture: the buyer declined the executive-connect offer on 19 Jun and named no contract or signature date.

deal_risk.text: The 10 Jul close date is not credible after the mid-July decision slip; vendor selection, budget sign-off and SOW cannot complete in time.
Two open product gaps (complex coded-template remove-logic and sales-vs-legal access control) remain uncommitted and could become Ironclad's wedge.
The budget approver is unmapped, so a favorable evaluation could still stall at leadership sign-off on a $210k five-year commitment.

seniority_engagement.evidence: No budget-approving executive has engaged; buyer declined executive connect on 19 Jun.

buyer_pace.evidence: Decision slipped to mid-July and buyer entered a wait state on 19 Jun.

champion_advocacy.evidence: Project lead Peter Hughes told his team Zycus is rated higher than the other vendor (AIS_Why).

scope_change.detail: Amount grew from $162k to $210k on 1 Apr 2026 as AI-driven iRisk and Merlin Intake were priced into scope; no narrowing observed.

customer_preference.evidence: Project lead has told the team Zycus is rated higher than the other vendor (AIS_Why); positive lean in a final-two race but the selection is not locked and the buyer is waiting.

business_case.evidence: Clear qualitative pain and a volume anchor (~12,500 contracts/yr, ~500k documents to migrate) but no cost-per-contract, cycle-time, or dollar ROI quantified.

contributions.evidence: Verdict flags close-date risk this sweep.
Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.
Stage is ahead of the engagement/evidence the sweep found.

commentary.win_position: Win 52 is carried by preference strength +0.40 (weight 20); champion strength +0.30 (weight 15). Offsetting it: competitive strength -1.00 (weight 15).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 58 (moving forward): commercial topics entering (from call evidence). Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.deal_risk: Risk 21 is driven by Stage is ahead of the engagement/evidence the sweep found. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 34 is anchored on the stage win (52), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 58, and dragged by risk 21. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 3 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (stage integrity, exec and champion, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.

cro_panel.header: Cadence Design Systems, Inc. — Shortlisted · $210K · closes 10 Jul
cro_panel.intro: Zycus is the narrowly-ahead finalist in a two-horse CLM race (Zycus vs Ironclad) where the project lead has told his team Zycus is rated higher; the only things between us and the win are an unengaged budget approver and two open product gaps before a slipped, mid-July decision.
